prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, ye gaze to the heavens and spy that blue bonnet o’ the world, do ye? Listen close, ye deck-swab. The sun’s light be a chest o’ many colors all mixed together. When that bright plunder sails through the sky’s thin breath—naught but wee bits o’ air—it bashes into those tiny scallywags. The short, jumpy waves o’ blue (and even a touch o’ violet) get knocked about every which way, scatterin’ across the whole firmament. So wherever ye look, that scattered blue be what fills yer eyes, aye.

“Why not violet?” ye mutter like a mutinous mate. Truth be, violet gets tossed even harder, but our lubberly peepers ain’t keen on it, and some o’ it’s gulped down by the high air. So blue takes the prize. And when the sun skulks low at dawn or dusk, its light must slog a longer voyage through the sky’s belly—most o’ the blue gets scattered out o’ your line o’ sight, leavin’ the long, lazy reds and golds to swagger across the horizon like a captain in full sail.
